1.pericope_label: Shout for Joy to the Lord
1.synthesis: “Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth! Serve the LORD with gladness!” (vv.1–2). The reason: “Know that the LORD, he is God! It is he who made us, and we are his; we are his people, and the sheep of his pasture” (v.3). Enter his gates with thanksgiving, “for the LORD is good; his steadfast love endures forever” (vv.4–5). The integrative thread is universal, joyful thanksgiving grounded in who God is: this beloved call to praise summons all the earth to glad worship, rooting joy in the truths that God alone is God, that he made us and we belong to him as his flock, and that his goodness and steadfast love endure forever.
1.historical_context: Psalm 100 (the “Jubilate”), titled “a Psalm for giving thanks” (title), is the climactic call to worship of the enthronement collection. Its summons to “all the earth” (v.1) is universal, and its grounds — God as Maker, owner, and shepherd of his people (v.3) — and the enduring steadfast love (v.5) sum up the Psalter’s theology of praise. The flock imagery echoes Psalm 95:7 and Psalm 23:1.
1.literary_structure: This compact psalm (vv.1–5) is structured by two calls to worship (vv.1–2, v.4), each followed by grounds: the knowledge of who God is (v.3) and the goodness and steadfast love of God (v.5). Its brevity and fullness make it the quintessential summons to thankful praise.
1.theological_application: Joyful worship is grounded in truth about God — that he is God, our Maker, and our Shepherd, and that his steadfast love endures forever (v.3, v.5). Glad service and thanksgiving are the fitting response of those who know they belong to God as the sheep of his pasture (Romans 12:1). The believer is summoned to enter God’s presence with thanksgiving and praise, never as a grim duty but as the overflow of knowing the good and faithful God to whom we belong (Philippians 4:4). Joyful worship is grounded in truth about God — that he is God, our Maker, and our Shepherd, and that his steadfast love endures forever (v.3, v.5). Glad service and thanksgiving are the fitting response of those who know they belong to God as the sheep of his pasture (Romans 12:1). The believer enters God’s presence with thanksgiving not as a grim duty but as the overflow of knowing the good and faithful God to whom we belong (Philippians 4:4).
1.christology: The God who “made us, and we are his… the sheep of his pasture” (v.3) is revealed in Christ, the Good Shepherd who calls his sheep his own (John 10:14) and through whom all things were made (John 1:3). His goodness and steadfast love that “endure forever” (v.5) are poured out fully in the Son (John 1:16–17). The universal call to thankful praise anticipates the worship of the redeemed flock gathered to the Lamb from all the earth, serving him with everlasting gladness (Revelation 7:9–10).
